name: Young mitochondria improved aged-mouse cognition and motor performance
summary: The most direct aging-function signal for mitochondrial delivery is an aged-mouse mitotherapy study.
statement: In aged mice, repeated intravenous administration of mitochondria isolated from young mice was associated with coexistence of young and aged mtDNA in tissues, increased ATP, reduced ROS, and significant improvement in cognitive and motor performance.
population_or_model: Aged male BALB/c mice receiving young-mouse liver mitochondria by tail-vein injection
time_horizon: Five injections once every two days, followed by biochemical and behavioral testing
quantitative_note: The study reports behavior-test group sizes of n = 10 per group and biochemical group sizes of n = 4 per group.
caveats: This is animal evidence and does not establish human safety, dosing, biodistribution, or efficacy.
The study does not resolve whether transferred mitochondria durably engraft or only produce transient tissue effects.
The delivery source, route, and compatibility assumptions may not translate to older humans.
